other_observers: Cole Wild; I heard about a Brown Pelican at this location seen for about 30 minutes on May 29 or May 30 by Sol Miller's uncle but I was unable to find it on June 1. I then heard about the bird again on June 10 from a resident named Nancy Cruz. It has since
specific_location: Rock Bridge Lake at Water Valley development about 1 mile south of Windsor center
description: Lark, bulky bird with about a 6-foot wing-span; massive dark grayish bill about 18 inches long, deeper at the base. Belly of the bird was pale gray with a grainy appearance (probably due to very fine mottling). This color was a bit darker on the chest. In flight, the outer flight feathers (primaries) were much darker gray (blackish). The hind neck was chestnut-brown, with a frostly central stripe extending down from the crown; much of the head was bright white, with a lateral white stripe extending down each side of the neck. The throat was a golden yellow color, apparently bare skin, extending down almost half way down the length of the long neck. At the base of the bare skin the neck was entirely chestnut-brown (a ring about 2 inches thick). Below this there was a diamond-shaped off-white/yellowish patch at the top of the chest. The upperparts were pale gray-brown, fairly uniform in color.  Overall shape was typical of a pelican. Size was about 70% the length of adjacent American White Pelicans, and overall, 50-60% of the mass.  Webbed feet were blackish.Behaviors: Soaring over water; floating on water; standing on a log.Call: nonePlumage: subadult
